name: dissect-hypervisor

metadata.name: dissect.hypervisor
metadata.summary: A Dissect module implementing parsers for various hypervisor disk, backup and configuration files

documentation:
# dissect.hypervisor

A Dissect module implementing parsers for various hypervisor disk, backup and configuration files. For more information,
please see [the documentation](https://docs.dissect.tools/en/latest/projects/dissect.hypervisor/index.html).

## Requirements

This project is part of the Dissect framework and requires Python.

Information on the supported Python versions can be found in the Getting Started section of [the documentation](https://docs.dissect.tools/en/latest/index.html#getting-started).

## Installation

`dissect.hypervisor` is available on [PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/dissect.hypervisor/).

```bash
pip install dissect.hypervisor
```

This module is also automatically installed if you install the `dissect` package.

## Build and test instructions

This project uses `tox` to build source and wheel distributions. Run the following command from the root folder to build
these:

```bash
tox -e build
```

The build artifacts can be found in the `dist/` directory.

`tox` is also used to run linting and unit tests in a self-contained environment. To run both linting and unit tests
using the default installed Python version, run:

```bash
tox
```

For a more elaborate explanation on how to build and test the project, please see [the
documentation](https://docs.dissect.tools/en/latest/contributing/tooling.html).

## Contributing

The Dissect project encourages any contribution to the codebase. To make your contribution fit into the project, please
refer to [the development guide](https://docs.dissect.tools/en/latest/contributing/developing.html).

## Copyright and license

Dissect is released as open source by Fox-IT (<https://www.fox-it.com>) part of NCC Group Plc
(<https://www.nccgroup.com>).

Developed by the Dissect Team (<dissect@fox-it.com>) and made available at <https://github.com/fox-it/dissect>.

License terms: AGPL3 (<https://www.gnu.org/licenses/agpl-3.0.html>). For more information, see the LICENSE file.

classes.name: dissect.hypervisor.util.vmtar.VisorTarInfo
documentation:
Implements TarInfo for use with Visor Tar files (vmtar).

The main difference is that file data is located at the end of the tar file, on
an offset specified in the header.

classes.name: dissect.hypervisor.disk.vhdx.VHDX
documentation:
Hyper-V VHDX implementation.

Supports fixed, dynamic and differencing VHDX files.

Currently, differencing VHDX files must be passed as a pathlib.Path object, with
the parent VHDX in the same directory, or the registered absolute directory.

classes.name: dissect.hypervisor.disk.hdd.StorageStream
documentation:
Stream implementation for HDD streams.

HDD files can exist of one or multiple streams, starting at consecutive offsets.
This class stitches all streams together into a single stream.

Args:

: streams: A list of {class}`Storage` and file-like object tuples.

HDS is the format for Parallels sparse disk files.

modules.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx
documentation: Reference:
- crypto-util
- libvmlibs.so

functions.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx._parse_dictionary
documentation: Parse a VMX dictionary.

functions.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx._parse_key_locator
documentation:
Parse a key locator from a string.

Key locators are string formatted data structures with a forward slash (/) separator. Each component is
prefixed with a type, followed by that types' specific data. Values between separators are url encoded.

Interally called `KeyLocator`.

functions.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx._split_list
documentation:
Parse a key locator list from a string.

Lists are wrapped by braces and separated by comma. They can contain nested lists/pairs,
so we need to separate at the correct nest level.

functions.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx._parse_crypto_dict
documentation:
Parse a crypto dict from a string.

Crypto dicts are encoded as `key=encoded_value:key=encoded_value`.

Internally called `CryptoDict`.

functions.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx._decrypt_hmac
documentation:
Decrypt and validate ciphertext.

First 16 bytes of the ciphertext are the IV and the last N bytes are the HMAC digest.
The cleartext is padded using PKCS#7.

functions.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx._create_cipher
documentation:
Create a cipher object.

Dynamic based on the available crypto module.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx.VMX.parse
documentation: Parse a VMX dictionary from a string.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx.VMX.encrypted
documentation:
Return whether this VMX is encrypted.

Encrypted VMXs will have both a `encryption.keySafe` and `encryption.data` value.
The `encryption.keySafe` is a string encoded `KeySafe`, which is made up of key locators.

For example:
    vmware:key/list/(pair/(phrase/phrase_id/phrase_content,hmac,data),pair/(.../...,...,...))

A KeySafe must be a list of Pairs. Each Pair has a wrapped key, an HMAC type and some encrypted data.
It's implementation specific how to unwrap a key. E.g. a phrase is just PBKDF2. The unwrapped key
can be used to unlock the encrypted Pair data. This will contain the final encryption key to decrypt
the data in `encryption.data`.

So, in summary, to unseal a KeySafe:
Parse KeySafe -> iterate pairs -> unlock Pair -> unwrap key (e.g. Phrase) -> decrypt Pair data -> parse dict

The terms for unwrapping, unlocking and unsealing are taken from VMware.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx.VMX.unlock_with_phrase
documentation:
Unlock this VMX in-place with a passphrase if it's encrypted.

This will load the KeySafe from the current dictionary and attempt to recover the encryption key
from it using the given passphrase. This key is used to decrypt the encrypted VMX data.

The dictionary is updated in-place with the encrypted VMX data.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx.VMX.disks
documentation: Return a list of paths to disk files

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx.KeySafe.unseal_with_phrase
documentation: Unseal this KeySafe with a passphrase and return the decrypted key.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx.KeySafe.from_text
documentation: Parse a KeySafe from a string.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx.Pair.has_phrase
documentation: Return whether this Pair is a Phrase pair.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx.Pair._unlock
documentation: Decrypt the data in this Pair.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx.Pair.unlock
documentation:
Helper method to unlock this Pair for various wrapped keys.

Currently only supports `Phrase`.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx.Pair.unlock_with_phrase
documentation: Unlock this Pair with a passphrase and return the decrypted data.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.vmx.Phrase.unwrap
documentation:
Unwrap/generate the encryption key for a given passphrase.

VMware calls this unwrapping, but really it's a KDF with the properties of this Phrase.

modules.name: dissect.hypervisor.disk.qcow2
documentation: References:
- https://github.com/qemu/qemu/blob/master/block/qcow2.c
- https://github.com/qemu/qemu/blob/master/docs/interop/qcow2.txt

classes.name: dissect.hypervisor.disk.qcow2.QCow2
documentation:
QCOW2 virtual disk implementation.

Supports both data-file and backing-file, but must be manually given as arguments.

If a data-file is required, it's required to manually pass a file like object
as the `data_file` argument.

A backing-file can optionally be skipped if `qcow2.ALLOW_NO_BACKING_FILE` is passed
as the `backing_file` argument. In this case, any reads from a backing file will result
in all null bytes being read.

classes.name: dissect.hypervisor.disk.qcow2.L2Table
documentation: Convenience class for accessing the L2 table.

classes.name: dissect.hypervisor.disk.qcow2.QCow2Snapshot
documentation: Wrapper class for snapshot table entries.

modules.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv
documentation: References:
- VmDataStore.dll

classes.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv.HyperVFile
documentation:
HyperVFile implementation.

I think, technically, the underlying container is called `HyperVStorage`, and the `HyperVFile` adds
some features on top of that. We just call it `HyperVFile` for convenience.

A `HyperVFile` has 2 headers, one at `0x0000` and one at `0x1000`. The active header is determined
by a sequence number.

A replay log is located at an offset specified in the header. This replay log functions as a journal
for changes made to the file. A file is dirty if it contains outstanding entries in the replay log,
and replaying the specified log entries is necessary. This is not currently implemented.

An object table seems to be located at `0x2000`, but it's unclear if this is always the case. The offset
might be related to the header size, log size or data alignment. This will need some more research.
This table contains entries that describe the various "objects" contained within this file. The available
types are listed in the `ObjectEntryType` enum.

`HyperVFile`'s have a version number, and it looks like there are some slight differences between
different versions. The key tables are at least stored in a different manner, because there's code
to handle loading them differently, and also to update them to the new format if an old version is
encountered. However, I haven't seen any files with older versions yet, so we guard this implementation
to only version `0x0400` at this time.

classes.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv.HyperVStorageReplayLog
documentation:
The replay log tracks changes in the file.

Old changes are actually still resident in the log, but not counted in the `num_entries` field.

classes.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv.HyperVStorageObjectTable
documentation:
The object table tracks all "objects".

Objects are specific blocks in the file that have been designated a specific type.
For example, the object table can list one or more key tables.

A table has a specific table index and one or more key entries.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv.HyperVStorageKeyTableEntry.type
documentation: Return the entry type.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv.HyperVStorageKeyTableEntry.size
documentation: Return the entry size.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv.HyperVStorageKeyTableEntry.is_file_object_pointer
documentation: Return whether the value is a file object pointer.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv.HyperVStorageKeyTableEntry.file_object_pointer
documentation: Return the file object pointer information.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv.HyperVStorageKeyTableEntry.raw
documentation: Returns the raw data for this entry.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv.HyperVStorageKeyTableEntry.data
documentation: Returns the data portion for this entry.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv.HyperVStorageKeyTableEntry.key
documentation: Returns the key name for this entry.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv.HyperVStorageKeyTableEntry.value
documentation: Return a Python native value for this entry.

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv.HyperVStorageKeyTableEntry.data_size
documentation:
Return the total amount of data bytes, including the key name.

Reference:
    - ``HyperVStorageKeyTableEntry::GetDataSizeInBytes``

methods.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv.HyperVStorageKeyTableEntry.value_size
documentation:
Return the amount of bytes a value occupies.

Reference:
    - ``HyperVStorageKeyTableEntry::GetValueSizeInBytes``

Entry in a key table.

The first 16 bytes are a combined flag and type field. The high 8 bits are flags, the low 8 bits are type.

Only one flag is currently known, which we've called `FileObjectPointer`. It's the lowest bit of the flag bits.
If this flag is set, it means the value of this entry is located in a file object. File objects pointers are
12 bytes and consist of a `uint32` size and a `uint64` offset value. The offset is the absolute offset into the
file. This method is similar to how parent keys are referenced.

Values are stored in a file object if their size >= `0x800`. As only strings and "arrays" are of variable size,
these are the only data types that can be stored in file objects.

Data type summary:

- `KeyDataType.Free`:
  : - Allocated but free.
- `KeyDataType.Unknown`:
  : - Unknown entry type. Anything greater than 9 and exactly 2 is unknown.
- `KeyDataType.Int`:
  : - Signed 64 bit integer.
- `KeyDataType.UInt`:
  : - Unsigned 64 bit integer.
- `KeyDataType.Double`:
  : - 64 bit double.
- `KeyDataType.String`:
  : - UTF-16-LE encoded string
- `KeyDataType.Array`:
  : - Bytes?
- `KeyDataType.Bool`:
  : - Boolean encoded as 32 bit integer.
- `KeyDataType.Node`:
  : - Tree nodes. Value size is coded as 8, but actual size is 12.
    - First 8 bytes are unknown, but latter 4 bytes is the insertion sequence number.

classes.name: dissect.hypervisor.descriptor.hyperv.HyperVStorageFileObject
documentation:
File object from the object table.

File objects are referenced by their absolute offset in the file. The object table also stores
a size, but this size will always be aligned with the data alignment of the Hyper-V file.
The actual size of the data stored is located in the data portion of the `HyperVStorageKeyTableEntry`
that references that file object.
